original_query: PFAS water filtration methods for municipal treatment

sections.title: Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) Filtration
sections.content: Granular activated carbon is one of the most effective and widely deployed technologies for PFAS removal in municipal water systems.

sections.title: Ion Exchange Resin Technology
sections.content: Ion exchange resins, particularly single-use variants, show high selectivity for short-chain PFAS compounds.

sections.title: Nanofiltration and Reverse Osmosis
sections.content: Membrane-based approaches provide near-complete PFAS removal but at higher operational cost.

evidence.statement: GAC removes greater than 90% of long-chain PFAS compounds including PFOA and PFOS at empty bed contact times of 10-20 minutes.
Bituminous coal-based GAC outperforms coconut shell GAC for PFAS adsorption due to larger pore structure.
GAC bed life for PFAS treatment ranges from 6 to 18 months depending on influent concentration and co-contaminants.
Single-use ion exchange resins achieve 95% removal of short-chain PFAS that breakthrough GAC systems.
Regenerable ion exchange resins reduce waste volume by 90% compared to single-use but have 10-15% lower removal efficiency.
Nanofiltration membranes with molecular weight cutoff below 300 Da reject 95-99% of all PFAS compounds.
Reverse osmosis achieves near-complete PFAS removal but generates concentrated brine requiring further treatment.
Combined GAC-nanofiltration treatment trains reduce total cost of ownership by 30% compared to RO-only systems.

bibliography.title: Water Research Foundation: IX for PFAS
